name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: Data loading, noise sensitivity analysis, and filtering validation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load both datasets from their exact paths
        - For point-vortex data: compute single-step velocity increments from `x_true` and `x_noisy` for all N configurations; fit power-law tails to the increment distributions using Hill estimator and compare tail index μ between true and noisy versions
        - Apply Savitzky-Golay filter to noisy trajectories with varying window sizes (3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 21 steps); for each window size compute the tail index μ of the filtered increments; plot μ vs window size to identify the plateau region
        - Compute basic summary statistics (mean, std, skewness, kurtosis) of increments for both true and noisy data across all N configurations
        - Save all computed results (tail indices, filtered increments, summary stats) to disk for use in subsequent steps
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) tail index μ vs window size for each N configuration, (b) comparison of increment PDFs (true vs noisy vs best-filtered) for N=20 and N=40, (c) summary table of noise impact on μ

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: Mechanistic residence time analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - For each tracer trajectory (all N configurations), compute instantaneous speed from true positions using finite differences
        - Define "trapped" state using a velocity threshold (e.g., speed below the 25th percentile of the speed distribution for that tracer) and identify contiguous trapped intervals
        - Calculate the distribution of residence times P(τ_res) for each N configuration by pooling across all tracers in that configuration
        - Fit power-law scaling P(τ_res) ~ τ_res^{-γ} to the tail of each residence time distribution using maximum likelihood estimation; report γ and its uncertainty for each N
        - Identify any exponential cutoffs in the residence time distributions and link them to the finite time horizon
        - Save residence time distributions and fitted parameters to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) log-log plots of P(τ_res) for each N with power-law fits overlaid, (b) γ vs N showing the trend, (c) example tracer speed time series with trapped intervals highlighted for N=5 and N=40

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: Velocity autocorrelation function (VACF) and memory/mutual information analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Compute the VACF C_v(Δt) for all point-vortex tracers (using true positions) for each N configuration; average over tracers within each N group
        - Compute the VACF for all Lévy walk trajectories for each β class; average over trajectories within each β group
        - Compute Mutual Information I(v(t), v(t+Δt)) as a function of lag Δt for both datasets using a histogram-based estimator; compare the decay rates between point-vortex and Lévy walk systems
        - Fit exponential and power-law decay models to the VACF tails; report which model fits better and the characteristic decay time/exponent for each N and β
        - Save all VACF and MI results to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) VACF vs lag time for all N configurations (point-vortex), (b) VACF vs lag time for all β classes (Lévy walk), (c) MI vs lag time for both datasets overlaid, (d) comparison of VACF decay exponents vs N and β

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Sliding-window non-stationarity and ergodicity-breaking analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Compute the Time-Averaged MSD (TAMSD) for each individual tracer trajectory using the standard formula; use lag times from 1 step up to 1/4 of the total trajectory length
        - Implement a sliding-window approach: divide each trajectory into overlapping windows of size W (choose W based on characteristic vortex interaction time); compute local anomalous exponent α(t) by fitting power-law to TAMSD within each window
        - Compute the ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δ) = Var(TAMSD(Δ)) / Mean(TAMSD(Δ))^2 as a function of lag time Δ for both datasets
        - Compare EB(Δ) profiles between point-vortex configurations and Lévy walk classes
        - Save TAMSD arrays, α(t) time series, and EB(Δ) profiles to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) TAMSD curves for all tracers per N configuration with ensemble average overlaid, (b) local α(t) vs time for each N, (c) EB(Δ) vs lag time for point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) on the same axes

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: MSD power-law fitting and Lévy walk null model comparison
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Compute ensemble-averaged MSD (EAMSD) from true positions for each N configuration (point-vortex) and each β class (Lévy walk) by averaging msd_true over all tracers/trajectories at each time step
        - Fit power-law α_emp to the EAMSD in the intermediate time range (excluding very early ballistic and very late saturation regimes) using log-log linear regression with uncertainty estimates
        - For each N configuration, compute the KL divergence between the velocity increment PDF of the point-vortex tracers and the best-fit Lévy-stable distribution; also compute KL divergence against each β class of Lévy walks to identify the best-matching Lévy walk class
        - Quantify VACF decay rate divergence between point-vortex and Lévy walk systems (from Step 3 results)
        - Construct the state-space map (α_emp, μ) for both datasets
        - Save all MSD fits, KL divergences, and state-space coordinates to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) EAMSD vs time for all N and β classes on log-log axes with power-law fits, (b) α_emp vs N (point-vortex) with Lévy walk α_theory values as horizontal reference lines, (c) KL divergence heatmap (N vs β) showing best-matching Lévy walk class

- Step 6:
      * sub-task: Phase-space mapping, Lévy-stable distribution fitting, and breakdown analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Fit Lévy-stable distributions to the velocity increment PDFs for all N configurations and all β classes using scipy.stats.levy_stable; extract stability index α_stable and skewness parameter β_stable
        - Construct the full (α_emp, μ) state-space map combining results from Steps 4 and 5; plot both point-vortex and Lévy walk datasets in this space
        - Compute the KL divergence between point-vortex increment PDFs and their best-matching Lévy walk increment PDFs as a function of N and time window (early vs late trajectory segments) to document where Hamiltonian "stickiness" dominates
        - Derive the theoretical prediction for α from vortex density: use the 1/r kernel argument to predict μ=2 (Cauchy) as baseline, then compute finite-N corrections using the empirical μ values; compare predicted vs empirical α
        - Save all Lévy-stable fit parameters, state-space coordinates, and KL divergence matrices to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) state-space map (α_emp vs μ) with both datasets, (b) KL divergence vs N showing breakdown of Lévy equivalence, (c) theoretical vs empirical α with finite-N correction curve, (d) velocity increment PDFs for N=40 vs best-matching Lévy walk with Lévy-stable fit overlaid

- Step 7:
      * sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–6 into a coherent Results section
        - Report noise sensitivity findings: how the 0.02 m noise floor affects tail index μ, the optimal Savitzky-Golay window size, and the plateau region
        - Report residence time distributions: power-law exponents γ for each N, evidence of cutoffs, and physical interpretation
        - Report VACF and mutual information results: decay rates, comparison between point-vortex and Lévy walk, evidence of Hamiltonian memory
        - Report sliding-window α(t) and EB(Δ) results: stationarity assessment, ergodicity-breaking comparison between datasets
        - Report MSD scaling results: α_emp vs N, comparison with Lévy walk α_theory, identification of best-matching Lévy walk class for each N
        - Report phase-space mapping results: (α_emp, μ) coordinates, KL divergence analysis, breakdown of Lévy equivalence, finite-N corrections to theoretical α prediction
        - Include all key numerical values, uncertainties, and references to the figures generated in previous steps

- Step 1:

	* instructions:

		- Compute basic summary statistics (mean, std, skewness, kurtosis) of increments for both true and noisy data across all N configurations
		- Save all computed results (tail indices, filtered increments, summary stats) to disk for use in subsequent steps
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) tail index μ vs window size for each N configuration, (b) comparison of increment PDFs (true vs noisy vs best-filtered) for N=20 and N=40, (c) summary table of noise impact on μ

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: Mechanistic residence time analysis

	* instructions:

		- Fit power-law scaling P(τ_res) ~ τ_res^{-γ} to the tail of each residence time distribution using maximum likelihood estimation; report γ and its uncertainty for each N
		- Identify any exponential cutoffs in the residence time distributions and link them to the finite time horizon
		- Save residence time distributions and fitted parameters to disk

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: Velocity autocorrelation function (VACF) and memory/mutual information analysis
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Compute the VACF C_v(Δt) for all point-vortex tracers (using true positions) for each N configuration; average over tracers within each N group
		- Compute the VACF for all Lévy walk trajectories for each β class; average over trajectories within each β group
		- Compute Mutual Information I(v(t), v(t+Δt)) as a function of lag Δt for both datasets using a histogram-based estimator; compare the decay rates between point-vortex and Lévy walk systems
		- Fit exponential and power-law decay models to the VACF tails; report which model fits better and the characteristic decay time/exponent for each N and β
		- Save all VACF and MI results to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) VACF vs lag time for all N configurations (point-vortex), (b) VACF vs lag time for all β classes (Lévy walk), (c) MI vs lag time for both datasets overlaid, (d) comparison of VACF decay exponents vs N and β

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Sliding-window non-stationarity and ergodicity-breaking analysis
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Compute the Time-Averaged MSD (TAMSD) for each individual tracer trajectory using the standard formula; use lag times from 1 step up to 1/4 of the total trajectory length
		- Implement a sliding-window approach: divide each trajectory into overlapping windows of size W (choose W based on characteristic vortex interaction time); compute local anomalous exponent α(t) by fitting power-law to TAMSD within each window
		- Compute the ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δ) = Var(TAMSD(Δ)) / Mean(TAMSD(Δ))^2 as a function of lag time Δ for both datasets
		- Compare EB(Δ) profiles between point-vortex configurations and Lévy walk classes
		- Save TAMSD arrays, α(t) time series, and EB(Δ) profiles to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) TAMSD curves for all tracers per N configuration with ensemble average overlaid, (b) local α(t) vs time for each N, (c) EB(Δ) vs lag time for point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) on the same axes

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: MSD power-law fitting and Lévy walk null model comparison
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Compute ensemble-averaged MSD (EAMSD) from true positions for each N configuration (point-vortex) and each β class (Lévy walk) by averaging msd_true over all tracers/trajectories at each time step
		- Fit power-law α_emp to the EAMSD in the intermediate time range (excluding very early ballistic and very late saturation regimes) using log-log linear regression with uncertainty estimates
		- For each N configuration, compute the KL divergence between the velocity increment PDF of the point-vortex tracers and the best-fit Lévy-stable distribution; also compute KL divergence against each β class of Lévy walks to identify the best-matching Lévy walk class
		- Quantify VACF decay rate divergence between point-vortex and Lévy walk systems (from Step 3 results)
		- Construct the state-space map (α_emp, μ) for both datasets

- Step 6:
	* sub-task: Phase-space mapping, Lévy-stable distribution fitting, and breakdown analysis

		- Fit Lévy-stable distributions to the velocity increment PDFs for all N configurations and all β classes using scipy.stats.levy_stable; extract stability index α_stable and skewness parameter β_stable
		- Construct the full (α_emp, μ) state-space map combining results from Steps 4 and 5; plot both point-vortex and Lévy walk datasets in this space

- Step 7:
	* sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper

		- Report VACF and mutual information results: decay rates, comparison between point-vortex and Lévy walk, evidence of Hamiltonian memory
		- Report sliding-window α(t) and EB(Δ) results: stationarity assessment, ergodicity-breaking comparison between datasets
		- Report MSD scaling results: α_emp vs N, comparison with Lévy walk α_theory, identification of best-matching Lévy walk class for each N
		- Report phase-space mapping results: (α_emp, μ) coordinates, KL divergence analysis, breakdown of Lévy equivalence, finite-N corrections to theoretical α prediction
		- Include all key numerical values, uncertainties, and references to the figures generated in previous steps

name: plan_reviewer
content:
**Recommendations:**

- Step 1 currently bundles data loading, noise sensitivity, filtering validation, and summary statistics into a single engineer step. Since the plan is already at 7 steps (below the 10-step maximum), consider splitting data loading and basic validation (confirming array shapes, field names, trajectory counts per configuration, and basic sanity checks) into a dedicated first engineer step, so that subsequent steps can import clean, verified data structures without repeating loading logic. This would make Step 1 strictly about noise sensitivity and filtering, improving clarity and reusability.

- In Step 1, the Hill estimator instruction should explicitly specify that the estimator must be applied only to the upper tail (absolute values of increments above a chosen threshold, e.g., the 90th percentile), and that the threshold sensitivity should be tested by varying the fraction of data used for the tail fit. Without this, the Hill estimator will produce unreliable μ estimates that contaminate all downstream comparisons.

- In Step 2, the velocity threshold for defining the "trapped" state is currently described as the 25th percentile of the per-tracer speed distribution. This per-tracer normalization means that even fast tracers will always have 25% of their time classified as "trapped," regardless of the actual physics. The instruction should instead specify a global threshold derived from the pooled speed distribution across all tracers within each N configuration, so that the trapped-state definition is physically meaningful and comparable across configurations.

- Step 3 asks for mutual information computed via a histogram-based estimator, but does not specify how to handle the normalization of velocities across configurations with very different speed scales (point-vortex vs. Lévy walk). The instruction should require that velocities be standardized (zero mean, unit variance) before computing MI, and that the number of histogram bins be chosen using a consistent rule (e.g., Sturges or Scott's rule) applied uniformly across all configurations and lag times to ensure comparability.

- Step 4 instructs the engineer to choose the sliding-window size W "based on characteristic vortex interaction time" without defining how to compute this quantity from the data. The instruction should specify a concrete operational definition, for example, the lag time at which the ensemble-averaged VACF first crosses zero (from Step 3 results), so that W is data-driven and reproducible rather than arbitrary.

- Step 5 currently asks for both EAMSD fitting and KL divergence computation in the same step, which is already computationally heavy. The KL divergence computation requires fitting Lévy-stable distributions (which is slow with scipy.stats.levy_stable), and this fitting is also requested again in Step 6. To avoid redundancy and potential inconsistency, Step 5 should use only the empirical increment histograms (not Lévy-stable fits) for the KL divergence computation, and reserve all Lévy-stable fitting exclusively for Step 6.

- Step 6 instructs the engineer to derive the theoretical prediction for α from vortex density using the 1/r kernel argument and finite-N corrections. This theoretical derivation should be made concrete: the instruction should specify that the finite-N correction is computed as a function of the empirical tail index μ(N) obtained from the Hill estimator in Step 1, using the relation α_theory(N) = 3 - μ(N), and that the predicted curve should be plotted alongside the empirical α_emp(N) from Step 4/5 with residuals reported.

- Step 7 (researcher) should explicitly instruct the researcher to organize the Results section into clearly labeled subsections corresponding to each analysis step (noise sensitivity, residence times, VACF/memory, ergodicity breaking, MSD scaling, phase-space mapping), and to include a summary table consolidating the key quantitative outputs (α_emp, μ, γ, EB plateau value, best-matching β class) for each N configuration, so that the paper writer agent receives a fully structured and self-contained document.

name: reviewer_response_evaluator
content:
**Recommendations:**

Split data loading and basic validation into a dedicated first engineer step to ensure subsequent steps import clean, verified data structures.

Specify in Step 1 that the Hill estimator must be applied only to the upper tail (e.g., 90th percentile) and require threshold sensitivity testing by varying the fraction of data used.

Replace the per-tracer velocity threshold in Step 2 with a global threshold derived from the pooled speed distribution across all tracers within each N configuration.

Require velocity standardization (zero mean, unit variance) and a consistent binning rule (e.g., Sturges or Scott's) for mutual information computation in Step 3.

Define the sliding-window size W in Step 4 using a concrete operational definition, such as the lag time at which the ensemble-averaged VACF first crosses zero.

Restrict Step 5 to empirical increment histograms for KL divergence and reserve all Lévy-stable fitting exclusively for Step 6 to avoid redundancy.

Specify the theoretical derivation in Step 6 using the relation α_theory(N) = 3 - μ(N) and require plotting the predicted curve against empirical results with residuals.

Instruct the researcher in Step 7 to organize the Results section by analysis step and include a summary table consolidating key quantitative outputs for each N configuration.

name: review_recorder
content:
Recommendations have been logged.
Number of feedback rounds left: 0.
Now, update the plan accordingly, planner!

name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: Data loading and basic validation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load both datasets from their exact absolute paths
        - Confirm array shapes, field names, dtype for each dataset
        - Count trajectories per N configuration (point-vortex) and per β class (Lévy walk)
        - Print basic sanity checks: time range, position range, msd_true range for each group
        - Save validated arrays as compressed numpy files to the output folder for clean import in subsequent steps
        - Print a concise summary table of all validation results to console

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: Noise sensitivity analysis and filtering validation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load the validated datasets saved in Step 1
        - Compute single-step velocity increments from both `x_true`/`y_true` and `x_noisy`/`y_noisy` for all N configurations; combine x and y increments into a single pool of absolute increments per configuration
        - Apply Hill estimator strictly to the upper tail (absolute increments above the 90th percentile); vary the tail fraction from 5% to 30% in steps to test threshold sensitivity; report μ as a function of tail fraction for both true and noisy data
        - Apply Savitzky-Golay filter to noisy trajectories with window sizes 3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 21 steps; for each window size compute the Hill tail index μ of the filtered increments using the same tail fraction identified as stable in the sensitivity test
        - Compute summary statistics (mean, std, skewness, kurtosis) of increments for true, noisy, and best-filtered data across all N configurations
        - Save all tail indices, filtered increment arrays, and summary statistics to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) μ vs tail fraction for true vs noisy data for each N, (b) μ vs Savitzky-Golay window size for each N to identify the plateau, (c) increment PDFs (true vs noisy vs best-filtered) for N=20 and N=40 on log-log axes

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: Mechanistic residence time analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - For each tracer trajectory compute instantaneous speed from true positions using finite differences
        - Define the "trapped" state using a global velocity threshold derived from the pooled speed distribution across all tracers within each N configuration (e.g., 25th percentile of the pooled speed); identify contiguous trapped intervals for each tracer
        - Calculate the distribution of residence times P(τ_res) for each N configuration by pooling across all tracers in that configuration
        - Fit power-law P(τ_res) ~ τ_res^{-γ} to the tail using maximum likelihood estimation; report γ and its uncertainty for each N; also test for exponential cutoffs
        - Save residence time distributions and fitted parameters to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) log-log plots of P(τ_res) for each N with power-law fits overlaid, (b) γ vs N, (c) example tracer speed time series with trapped intervals highlighted for N=5 and N=40

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Velocity autocorrelation function and mutual information analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Compute VACF C_v(Δt) for all point-vortex tracers (true positions) for each N configuration; average over tracers within each N group; compute up to lag = half the trajectory length
        - Compute VACF for all Lévy walk trajectories for each β class; average over trajectories within each β group
        - Identify the lag time at which the ensemble-averaged VACF first crosses zero for each N configuration; save these zero-crossing times as the characteristic interaction times for use as window size W in Step 5
        - Standardize velocities (zero mean, unit variance) within each configuration before computing Mutual Information I(v(t), v(t+Δt)) as a function of lag Δt using a histogram-based estimator with Scott's rule for bin count; apply uniformly across all configurations and lag times
        - Fit exponential and power-law decay models to the VACF tails; report which model fits better and the characteristic decay time/exponent for each N and β
        - Save all VACF, zero-crossing times, and MI results to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) VACF vs lag time for all N (point-vortex), (b) VACF vs lag time for all β (Lévy walk), (c) MI vs lag time for both datasets overlaid, (d) VACF decay exponents vs N and β

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: Sliding-window non-stationarity and ergodicity-breaking analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load VACF zero-crossing times from Step 4; use the zero-crossing lag time for each N configuration as the sliding-window size W
        - Compute TAMSD for each individual tracer trajectory using lag times from 1 step up to 1/4 of total trajectory length
        - Implement sliding-window analysis: divide each trajectory into overlapping windows of size W; compute local anomalous exponent α(t) by fitting power-law to TAMSD within each window
        - Compute ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δ) = Var(TAMSD(Δ)) / Mean(TAMSD(Δ))^2 as a function of lag time Δ for both point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) datasets
        - Save TAMSD arrays, α(t) time series, and EB(Δ) profiles to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) TAMSD curves for all tracers per N with ensemble average overlaid, (b) local α(t) vs time for each N, (c) EB(Δ) vs lag time for all N and all β on the same axes

- Step 6:
      * sub-task: MSD power-law fitting and Lévy walk null model comparison using empirical KL divergence
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Compute ensemble-averaged MSD (EAMSD) from msd_true for each N configuration and each β class by averaging over all tracers/trajectories at each time step
        - Fit power-law α_emp to the EAMSD in the intermediate time range (excluding early ballistic and late saturation regimes) using log-log linear regression; report α_emp and uncertainty for each N and β
        - Compute empirical velocity increment PDFs (using absolute increments, consistent binning with Scott's rule) for all N configurations and all β classes; use these empirical histograms (no Lévy-stable fitting) to compute KL divergence between each point-vortex N and each Lévy walk β class; identify the best-matching β for each N
        - Construct the state-space map (α_emp, μ) using α_emp from EAMSD fits and μ from Hill estimator (Step 2) for both datasets
        - Save all EAMSD fits, KL divergence matrices, and state-space coordinates to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) EAMSD vs time for all N and β on log-log axes with power-law fits, (b) α_emp vs N with Lévy walk α_theory as horizontal reference lines, (c) KL divergence heatmap (N vs β)

- Step 7:
      * sub-task: Phase-space mapping, Lévy-stable fitting, theoretical α prediction, and breakdown analysis
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Fit Lévy-stable distributions to velocity increment PDFs for all N configurations and all β classes using scipy.stats.levy_stable; extract stability index α_stable and skewness β_stable
        - Construct the full (α_emp, μ) state-space map combining EAMSD α_emp from Step 6 and Hill μ from Step 2; plot both point-vortex and Lévy walk datasets in this space
        - Compute KL divergence between point-vortex increment PDFs and their best-matching Lévy walk increment PDFs as a function of N and time window (early vs late trajectory segments) to document where Hamiltonian stickiness dominates
        - Derive theoretical α prediction: use μ=2 (Cauchy baseline from 1/r kernel) and finite-N correction via α_theory(N) = 3 - μ(N) where μ(N) is the empirical Hill estimate from Step 2; plot predicted curve vs empirical α_emp(N) from Step 6 and report residuals
        - Save all Lévy-stable fit parameters, state-space coordinates, and KL divergence matrices to disk
        - Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) state-space map (α_emp vs μ) with both datasets, (b) KL divergence vs N (early vs late windows), (c) theoretical vs empirical α with finite-N correction curve and residuals, (d) increment PDFs for N=40 vs best-matching Lévy walk with Lévy-stable fit overlaid

- Step 8:
      * sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–7 into a coherent Results section organized into clearly labeled subsections corresponding to each analysis: noise sensitivity and filtering, residence time distributions, VACF and memory effects, ergodicity breaking, MSD scaling and Lévy walk comparison, and phase-space mapping
        - Report noise sensitivity findings: how the 0.02 m noise floor affects tail index μ for each N, the optimal Savitzky-Golay window size identified from the plateau, and the quantitative difference in μ between true and noisy data
        - Report residence time distributions: power-law exponents γ and uncertainties for each N, evidence of exponential cutoffs, and physical interpretation in terms of vortex trapping
        - Report VACF and mutual information results: decay rates and model fits for each N and β, VACF zero-crossing times used as window sizes, comparison between point-vortex and Lévy walk memory structures
        - Report sliding-window α(t) and EB(Δ) results: stationarity assessment, EB plateau values for each N and β, comparison of ergodicity breaking between datasets
        - Report MSD scaling results: α_emp values and uncertainties for each N, comparison with Lévy walk α_theory, best-matching β class for each N from KL divergence analysis
        - Report phase-space mapping results: (α_emp, μ) coordinates for all configurations, Lévy-stable fit parameters, KL divergence breakdown by time window, finite-N correction residuals, and the theoretical α_theory(N) = 3 - μ(N) prediction vs empirical values
        - Include a summary table consolidating key quantitative outputs (α_emp, μ, γ, EB plateau, best-matching β) for each N configuration
        - Reference all figures generated in previous steps

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:
**PLAN**

- Step 1:
	* sub-task: Data loading and basic validation
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load both datasets from their exact absolute paths
		- Confirm array shapes, field names, dtype for each dataset
		- Count trajectories per N configuration (point-vortex) and per β class (Lévy walk)
		- Print basic sanity checks: time range, position range, msd_true range for each group
		- Save validated arrays as compressed numpy files to the output folder for clean import in subsequent steps
		- Print a concise summary table of all validation results to console

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: Noise sensitivity analysis and filtering validation
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load the validated datasets saved in Step 1
		- Compute single-step velocity increments from both x_true/y_true and x_noisy/y_noisy for all N configurations; combine x and y increments into a single pool of absolute increments per configuration
		- Apply Hill estimator strictly to the upper tail (absolute increments above the 90th percentile); vary the tail fraction from 5% to 30% in steps to test threshold sensitivity; report μ as a function of tail fraction for both true and noisy data
		- Apply Savitzky-Golay filter to noisy trajectories with window sizes 3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 21 steps; for each window size compute the Hill tail index μ of the filtered increments using the same tail fraction identified as stable in the sensitivity test
		- Compute summary statistics (mean, std, skewness, kurtosis) of increments for true, noisy, and best-filtered data across all N configurations
		- Save all tail indices, filtered increment arrays, and summary statistics to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) μ vs tail fraction for true vs noisy data for each N, (b) μ vs Savitzky-Golay window size for each N to identify the plateau, (c) increment PDFs (true vs noisy vs best-filtered) for N=20 and N=40 on log-log axes

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: Mechanistic residence time analysis
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- For each tracer trajectory compute instantaneous speed from true positions using finite differences
		- Define the "trapped" state using a global velocity threshold derived from the pooled speed distribution across all tracers within each N configuration (e.g., 25th percentile of the pooled speed); identify contiguous trapped intervals for each tracer
		- Calculate the distribution of residence times P(τ_res) for each N configuration by pooling across all tracers in that configuration
		- Fit power-law P(τ_res) ~ τ_res^{-γ} to the tail using maximum likelihood estimation; report γ and its uncertainty for each N; also test for exponential cutoffs
		- Save residence time distributions and fitted parameters to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) log-log plots of P(τ_res) for each N with power-law fits overlaid, (b) γ vs N, (c) example tracer speed time series with trapped intervals highlighted for N=5 and N=40

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Velocity autocorrelation function and mutual information analysis
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Compute VACF C_v(Δt) for all point-vortex tracers (true positions) for each N configuration; average over tracers within each N group; compute up to lag = half the trajectory length
		- Compute VACF for all Lévy walk trajectories for each β class; average over trajectories within each β group
		- Identify the lag time at which the ensemble-averaged VACF first crosses zero for each N configuration; save these zero-crossing times as the characteristic interaction times for use as window size W in Step 5
		- Standardize velocities (zero mean, unit variance) within each configuration before computing Mutual Information I(v(t), v(t+Δt)) as a function of lag Δt using a histogram-based estimator with Scott's rule for bin count; apply uniformly across all configurations and lag times
		- Fit exponential and power-law decay models to the VACF tails; report which model fits better and the characteristic decay time/exponent for each N and β
		- Save all VACF, zero-crossing times, and MI results to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) VACF vs lag time for all N (point-vortex), (b) VACF vs lag time for all β (Lévy walk), (c) MI vs lag time for both datasets overlaid, (d) VACF decay exponents vs N and β

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Sliding-window non-stationarity and ergodicity-breaking analysis
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load VACF zero-crossing times from Step 4; use the zero-crossing lag time for each N configuration as the sliding-window size W
		- Compute TAMSD for each individual tracer trajectory using lag times from 1 step up to 1/4 of total trajectory length
		- Implement sliding-window analysis: divide each trajectory into overlapping windows of size W; compute local anomalous exponent α(t) by fitting power-law to TAMSD within each window
		- Compute ergodicity-breaking parameter EB(Δ) = Var(TAMSD(Δ)) / Mean(TAMSD(Δ))^2 as a function of lag time Δ for both point-vortex (all N) and Lévy walk (all β) datasets
		- Save TAMSD arrays, α(t) time series, and EB(Δ) profiles to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) TAMSD curves for all tracers per N with ensemble average overlaid, (b) local α(t) vs time for each N, (c) EB(Δ) vs lag time for all N and all β on the same axes

- Step 6:
	* sub-task: MSD power-law fitting and Lévy walk null model comparison using empirical KL divergence
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Compute ensemble-averaged MSD (EAMSD) from msd_true for each N configuration and each β class by averaging over all tracers/trajectories at each time step
		- Fit power-law α_emp to the EAMSD in the intermediate time range (excluding early ballistic and late saturation regimes) using log-log linear regression; report α_emp and uncertainty for each N and β
		- Compute empirical velocity increment PDFs (using absolute increments, consistent binning with Scott's rule) for all N configurations and all β classes; use these empirical histograms (no Lévy-stable fitting) to compute KL divergence between each point-vortex N and each Lévy walk β class; identify the best-matching β for each N
		- Construct the state-space map (α_emp, μ) using α_emp from EAMSD fits and μ from Hill estimator (Step 2) for both datasets
		- Save all EAMSD fits, KL divergence matrices, and state-space coordinates to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) EAMSD vs time for all N and β on log-log axes with power-law fits, (b) α_emp vs N with Lévy walk α_theory as horizontal reference lines, (c) KL divergence heatmap (N vs β)

- Step 7:
	* sub-task: Phase-space mapping, Lévy-stable fitting, theoretical α prediction, and breakdown analysis
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Fit Lévy-stable distributions to velocity increment PDFs for all N configurations and all β classes using scipy.stats.levy_stable; extract stability index α_stable and skewness β_stable
		- Construct the full (α_emp, μ) state-space map combining EAMSD α_emp from Step 6 and Hill μ from Step 2; plot both point-vortex and Lévy walk datasets in this space
		- Compute KL divergence between point-vortex increment PDFs and their best-matching Lévy walk increment PDFs as a function of N and time window (early vs late trajectory segments) to document where Hamiltonian stickiness dominates
		- Derive theoretical α prediction: use μ=2 (Cauchy baseline from 1/r kernel) and finite-N correction via α_theory(N) = 3 - μ(N) where μ(N) is the empirical Hill estimate from Step 2; plot predicted curve vs empirical α_emp(N) from Step 6 and report residuals
		- Save all Lévy-stable fit parameters, state-space coordinates, and KL divergence matrices to disk
		- Produce a multi-panel figure: (a) state-space map (α_emp vs μ) with both datasets, (b) KL divergence vs N (early vs late windows), (c) theoretical vs empirical α with finite-N correction curve and residuals, (d) increment PDFs for N=40 vs best-matching Lévy walk with Lévy-stable fit overlaid

- Step 8:
	* sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
	* agent in charge: researcher

	* instructions:
		- Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–7 into a coherent Results section organized into clearly labeled subsections corresponding to each analysis: noise sensitivity and filtering, residence time distributions, VACF and memory effects, ergodicity breaking, MSD scaling and Lévy walk comparison, and phase-space mapping
		- Report noise sensitivity findings: how the 0.02 m noise floor affects tail index μ for each N, the optimal Savitzky-Golay window size identified from the plateau, and the quantitative difference in μ between true and noisy data
		- Report residence time distributions: power-law exponents γ and uncertainties for each N, evidence of exponential cutoffs, and physical interpretation in terms of vortex trapping
		- Report VACF and mutual information results: decay rates and model fits for each N and β, VACF zero-crossing times used as window sizes, comparison between point-vortex and Lévy walk memory structures
		- Report sliding-window α(t) and EB(Δ) results: stationarity assessment, EB plateau values for each N and β, comparison of ergodicity breaking between datasets
		- Report MSD scaling results: α_emp values and uncertainties for each N, comparison with Lévy walk α_theory, best-matching β class for each N from KL divergence analysis
		- Report phase-space mapping results: (α_emp, μ) coordinates for all configurations, Lévy-stable fit parameters, KL divergence breakdown by time window, finite-N correction residuals, and the theoretical α_theory(N) = 3 - μ(N) prediction vs empirical values
		- Include a summary table consolidating key quantitative outputs (α_emp, μ, γ, EB plateau, best-matching β) for each N configuration
		- Reference all figures generated in previous steps

name: plan_recorder
content: Plan recorded as final. Planning stage complete.
